questions.q: A disgruntled employee uses their manager's login details, without permission, to read confidential files they are not allowed to see. Which law and offence is the most direct fit?
options: Data Protection Act 2018 — unfair processing
Computer Misuse Act 1990 — Offence 1 (unauthorised access)
Copyright, Designs & Patents Act 1988 — infringement
Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000 — unlawful interception
questions.explain: Gaining access to a system or data you are not authorised to use is CMA 1990 Offence 1 (unauthorised access to computer material). No further crime or modification is described, so Offences 2 and 3 do not apply.

questions.q: An online retailer is found to be storing customers' names, addresses and card details in an unencrypted database, and keeping records of customers who left years ago. Which law has it most clearly breached?
options: Computer Misuse Act 1990
Copyright, Designs & Patents Act 1988
Data Protection Act 2018 (+ GDPR)
Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000
questions.explain: This concerns an organisation mishandling personal data it holds: failing the security (integrity & confidentiality) and storage-limitation principles. That is the Data Protection Act 2018, enforced by the ICO.

questions.q: A student downloads a cracked copy of a paid video-editing program and shares it on a forum for others to install free of charge. Which law applies?
options: Copyright, Designs & Patents Act 1988
Computer Misuse Act 1990
Data Protection Act 2018
Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000
questions.explain: Software is an original work protected automatically by the Copyright, Designs & Patents Act 1988. Pirating and distributing it without a licence infringes the rights-holder's control over copying and distribution.

questions.q: During a serious criminal investigation, police obtain proper authorisation and require an ISP to provide access to a suspect's communications. Which law governs this?
options: Data Protection Act 2018
Computer Misuse Act 1990
Copyright, Designs & Patents Act 1988
Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000 (RIPA)
questions.explain: RIPA 2000 regulates how public bodies (police, security services) may lawfully intercept communications and compel ISPs to assist, and sets out who may authorise such interception to prevent abuse.

questions.q: A hacker breaks into a company's network and then writes a script that deletes large numbers of customer records. Which CMA offence best describes the deletion of the records?
options: Offence 1 — unauthorised access
Offence 2 — access with intent to commit a further offence
Offence 3 — unauthorised modification of computer material
No CMA offence — this is purely a Data Protection matter
questions.explain: Deleting or altering data without authorisation is CMA 1990 Offence 3 (unauthorised modification). Releasing malware or ransomware also falls under this offence.

questions.q: A hacker breaks into a firm that had stored its data unencrypted and steals that data. Which statement is correct about which laws apply?
options: Only the Computer Misuse Act applies
Only the Data Protection Act applies
Both can apply: CMA to the hacker, DPA to the firm's poor security
Neither applies because no money was stolen
questions.explain: Two laws can apply to one situation. The hacker commits a CMA offence by gaining unauthorised access, while the firm may breach the DPA's security principle by failing to protect the personal data it held.

questions.q: An inventor has created a genuinely novel hardware mechanism and wants legally enforceable, exclusive rights to the invention itself. What protection do they need, and how is it obtained?
options: Copyright — it is automatic with no registration
A patent — it must be applied for and granted
Data Protection registration with the ICO
A RIPA authorisation
questions.explain: Copyright is automatic and protects the expression of a work, not an invention. To protect an invention itself the inventor needs a patent, which (unlike copyright) must be formally applied for and granted.

questions.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes GDPR from the Data Protection Act 2018?
options: They are identical names for the same single statute
GDPR is the EU regulation; the DPA 2018 is the UK law implementing it
The DPA 2018 is the EU regulation and GDPR is UK-only
GDPR replaced the DPA 2018 and the DPA no longer exists
questions.explain: GDPR is the EU's General Data Protection Regulation, while the DPA 2018 is the UK legislation that implements and works alongside it. They are related but not the same thing.

questions.q: A factory replaces most of its production line with robots. Which is a genuine DRAWBACK most associated with this automation?
options: Higher long-term labour costs
Displacement of low-skilled workers and deskilling
Lower productivity and output
Reduced consistency of the product
questions.explain: Automation typically raises productivity and lowers cost, but its key social drawback is job displacement of low-skilled workers and deskilling of the remaining workforce.

questions.q: A bank uses an AI model trained on historical lending data to approve or reject loans automatically. Which concern is the clearest ETHICAL risk of this approach?
options: It processes applications too slowly
The model may reproduce or amplify bias in the training data and be a hard-to-explain 'black box'
It is incapable of handling large volumes of data
It guarantees every applicant is treated identically and fairly
questions.explain: Automated decision making and AI learn from historical data, so they can reproduce or amplify existing bias. The 'black box' nature also makes decisions hard to explain or appeal, raising accountability concerns.

questions.q: Which option best describes the 'digital divide'?
options: The energy gap between data centres and homes
The gap between those with access to technology and skills and those without
The split between proprietary and open-source software
The difference between copyright and patents
questions.explain: The digital divide is the inequality between people who have access to technology and the skills to use it and those who do not, often excluding poorer, rural, elderly or disabled groups and widening inequality.

questions.q: A company moves its customer-support call centre and software development overseas to reduce wage costs. This practice is best described as:
options: Offshoring
Open-sourcing
Censorship
Caching
questions.explain: Offshoring is moving work abroad to cut costs. Benefits include cheaper labour and 'follow the sun' coverage; drawbacks include local job losses and quality, time-zone and data-security concerns.

questions.q: A government orders ISPs to block access to certain websites it deems undesirable. Which pairing of benefit and drawback best fits this censorship?
options: Benefit: protects children/blocks illegal content; Drawback: can restrict free speech and silence dissent
Benefit: lowers e-waste; Drawback: increases data-centre energy
Benefit: prevents vendor lock-in; Drawback: stifles competition
Benefit: faster processing; Drawback: race conditions
questions.explain: Censorship can protect children and block illegal or extremist material, but it can also restrict free speech and be abused by states to silence dissent. These are the standard trade-offs for this issue.

questions.q: When answering a 12-mark 'discuss/evaluate' question on a social or ethical issue, what is required to reach the top level of response?
options: A long one-sided list of points with no conclusion
Only the benefits, stated in detail
A balanced argument covering benefits and drawbacks for multiple stakeholders, ending in a justified conclusion
A definition of every relevant law with no evaluation
questions.explain: Level 3 answers are balanced, consider multiple stakeholders and both benefits and drawbacks, use accurate terms, and finish with a justified conclusion that takes a clear position. A one-sided list cannot reach the top band.
